acquirers.name: LITTLE COMPANY OF MARY HEALTH CARE LIMITED

deals_cleared.merger_description: Little Company of Mary Health Care Ltd (**Calvary**) proposes to acquire the business and assets of Holmesglen Private Hospital (**Holmesglen Private**) from Healthscope.

events.title: Calvary - Holmesglen Private Hospital - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Little Company of Mary Health Care Ltd (Calvary) proposes to
acquire 100% of the assets of Holmesglen Private Hospital
(Holmesglen Private) from Healthscope pursuant to a Business and
Asset Sale Agreement (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Calvary is a not-for-profit, Catholic operator of public
and private hospitals in Australia, with facilities in Victoria, New
South Wales, South Australia, Tasmania, and the ACT.
The target, Holmesglen Private, is a private hospital in Melbourne,
Victoria which is currently operated by Healthscope.
In May 2025, Healthscope’s parent entities went into voluntary
administration, and receivers and administrators were appointed by
its lenders and the Healthscope Board respectively to conduct an
orderly sale of the hospitals in its network.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Calvary is a private and public hospital operator, and Holmesglen
Private is a private hospital located in Moorabbin, Victoria.
Calvary does not currently have any private hospitals in Victoria.
Calvary operates one publicly-funded healthcare facility in Victoria,
Calvary Health Care Bethlehem, located in the Melbourne suburb of
Caulfield South, a 7km drive from Holmesglen Private. Calvary
Bethlehem provides specialist healthcare services for patients with
progressive neurodegenerative disease, and for palliative care more
broadly.
Holmesglen Private is a private hospital focused on acute medical
and surgical care. Holmesglen Private does not have a palliative care
unit.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and information from third parties, including
submissions expressing concerns that post-acquisition, Calvary, as
a Catholic healthcare provider, is likely to discontinue the supply of
some services, including certain reproductive procedures, at
Holmesglen Private.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
There is limited horizontal overlap between Holmesglen Private and
Calvary Bethlehem, and they are not close competitors. Calvary
Bethlehem is a public facility principally supplying palliative care
services and treatment for progressive neurodegenerative disease,
and it does not have operating theatres or provide acute care.
Conversely, Holmesglen Private is a private hospital primarily
providing acute medical and surgical care, and it does not have a
palliative care unit.
Holmesglen Private would likely continue to face competition from
alternative suppliers of private hospital services in Melbourne.
The ACCC considers that following the Acquisition, it is likely that
Calvary, as a Catholic healthcare provider, will not provide the same
breadth of services that Holmesglen Private currently provides and
that certain services may not continue to be available at Holmesglen
Private in the future. The ACCC has had regard to the future without
the Acquisition, including the commercially likely scenarios for future
ownership and operation of Holmesglen Private, and considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially
lessening competition in any market.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Tega and Apollo Funds consortium - Molycop

acquirers.name: Tega MC SG Holdings Pte. Ltd.
Tega MC Australia Holdings Pty Ltd

targets.name: Molycop Group, comprising all of the entities owned directly or indirectly by AIP MC Holdings LLC.

deals_cleared.merger_description: The proposed acquisition involves a consortium comprised of Tega Industries Limited (**Tega**) and the Apollo funds indirectly and collectively acquiring all outstanding shares in the subsidiaries of AIP MC Holdings LLC that collectively form Molycop.

events.title: Tega and Apollo Funds consortium - Molycop - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
A consortium comprised of Tega Industries Limited (Tega) and funds
advised and/or managed by affiliates of certain subsidiaries of Apollo
Global Management, Inc (Apollo Funds) proposed acquisition of all
outstanding shares in the subsidiaries of AIP MC Holdings LLC that
collectively form Molycop (together, Molycop) (the Acquisition)]

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined
under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer is a consortium comprising Tega and the Apollo Funds.
Tega is a supplier of mining products such as mill and scrubber liners,
trommels, screen media, hydrocyclones, slurry pumps and parts,
conveyor components, chutes, and other rubber and composite wear
parts. Tega also provides installation, condition monitoring, and
maintenance services.
Apollo is an alternative asset management firm headquartered in New
York. Examples of current investments include companies in natural
resources, manufacturing and industrial, education, insurance, financial
services, and leisure businesses.
The target, Molycop, is a global supplier of mining consumables, with a
focus on the manufacture and sale of grinding media and flotation
chemicals for use in both semi-autogenous grinding mills and ball mills.
These products are used for mineral extraction and processing of
copper, gold, and iron ore. In Australia, Molycop also supplies
engineered products for the Australian rail and manufacturing
industries.

Molycop and Tega supply complementary products. Molycop’s grinding
media and Tega’s mill liners are both inputs used by mining customers
in grinding mills to process ore. Grinding media are fed into grinding
mills together with ore. The rotation of the grinding mill creates impact
between the grinding media and ore, reducing the ore material down to a
finer size and liberating the minerals of value.
Key types of grinding media supplied by Molycop include:
• SAG balls: large, forged steel balls that operate with a semi-
autogenous (SAG) mill – the first stage of grinding ore after
crushing.
• Small / medium balls: smaller forged steel balls that operate a
ball mill – the second phase of grinding ore (after the SAG mill).
• High-chrome balls: typically smaller balls (also used in a ball
mill) with a higher chromium content, that have a lower wear
rate than forged steel but are more susceptible to breakage.
Mill liners, such as those supplied by Tega, are customised wear parts
that fit the interior grinding mills and ball mills to protect internal
surfaces from wear and impact during grinding to extend a mill’s life and
improve operational efficiency.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to
be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance
with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth)
(the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the
Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified
acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim
merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the
information and documents that were submitted with the notification
form and information from third parties, and publicly available
information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as
it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of
substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its
decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the
following findings:
• There are no horizontal overlaps or material vertical relationships
between the parties.
• The ACCC has considered the potential for conglomerate effects,
particularly whether Tega would have the ability and incentive to
leverage Molycop’s strong market position in grinding media to
foreclose rival suppliers of mill liners by anti-competitively bundling
or tying the two products. However, the ACCC found:
− Many mining customers would be able to switch to a different
supplier of grinding media in response to any tying or bundling
strategy. While Molycop is the only supplier of Australian-made
grinding media and has a very high market share, they are
relatively commoditised products and customers are able to
switch to imported products.
− There would be practical barriers to bundling or tying the
products. For example, customers typically procure mill liners
and grinding media separately through different methods (spot
purchases for mill liners and competitive tenders for grinding
media) and over different time periods (grinding media are
typically supplied under longer term supply arrangements).

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the
Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act,
applications for review of the determination are to be made to the
Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after
this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions
Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review,
please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Bendigo and Adelaide Bank – RACQ Bank

targets.name: MEMBERS BANKING GROUP LIMITED (TRADING AS RACQ BANK)

deals_cleared.merger_description: Bendigo and Adelaide Bank Limited (**Bendigo and Adelaide Bank**) and Members Banking Group Limited trading as RACQ Bank (**RACQ Bank**) have entered into an agreement for Bendigo and Adelaide Bank to acquire RACQ Bank’s retail loan and deposit books by way of an asset sale (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Bendigo and Adelaide Bank Limited (Bendigo and Adelaide Bank)
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of Members Banking Group Limited’s, trading as RACQ
Bank (RACQ Bank), retail loan and deposit books by way of an asset
sale, as described in the transaction documents provided as part of
the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
28 April 2026

The acquirer, Bendigo and Adelaide Bank, is an Australian, ASX-listed
(ASX:BEN) authorised deposit-taking institution (ADI) headquartered
in Bendigo, Victoria. Established in 1858, Bendigo and Adelaide Bank
provides retail banking services to over 2.9m customers through 416
branches across Australia, and has over A$73bn of customer
deposits and over A$84bn of total lending. Bendigo and Adelaide
Bank’s customer-facing banking business is split into a consumer
division and a business and agribusiness division.
The target, RACQ Bank, is an ADI and wholly-owned indirect
subsidiary of The Royal Automobile Club of Queensland Limited
(RACQ). RACQ Bank provides retail loan and deposit banking
services, including personal, home and business loans, transactions
accounts, savings accounts and term deposits to approximately
90,000 customers. At 30 June 2025, RACQ Bank’s retail business
comprised A$2.7bn of retail loans and A$2.5bn of retail deposits.
The parties both supply retail lending and retail deposit products in
Queensland and across Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. while there is a horizontal overlap between Bendigo and
Adelaide Bank and RACQ Bank in the supply of retail lending
and retail deposit products in Queensland and nationally,
their combined share in the supply of these products in each
would be low, and the increase in share of supply arising
from the Acquisition would be small
b. there are alternative suppliers of retail lending and retail
deposit products in Queensland and nationally.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Allianz Australia Services Pty Limited (Allianz Australia Services)
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
arrangements with Lendlease (Victoria Cross) Pty Limited as trustee
for Lendlease Victoria Cross Trust (Lendlease (Victoria Cross)) and
Victoria Cross Commercial Pty Ltd as trustee for Victoria Cross
Commercial Trust (Victoria Cross Commercial) for the lease of
commercial office space on levels 16 and 17 of the new Victoria
Cross Tower development located at 155 Miller Street, North Sydney
NSW, as described in the transaction documents provided as part of
the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
28 April 2026

The acquirer, Allianz Australia Services is a wholly owned subsidiary
of Allianz Australia Insurance Limited (Allianz).
Allianz offers a range of insurance products and services for
individuals and business, including car, home, landlord, travel, life,
boat and workers' compensation insurance.
Allianz intends to use the Premises as commercial office space from
which to conduct its insurance business.
The vendor, Lendlease (Victoria Cross), is the developer of the new
Victoria Cross Tower development in North Sydney. Victoria Cross
Commercial is the proposed lessor of the premises.
Both Lendlease (Victoria Cross) and Victoria Cross Commercial are
wholly owned subsidiaries of Lendlease Corporation Limited
(Lendlease).
Lendlease is an Australian real estate and investment business,
which uses an integrated model of investments, development and
construction to create and manage mixed-use precincts and civic
and social infrastructure.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal competitive overlap between the
parties
b. there are alternative sites available for Allianz’s competitors,
and the Acquisition is not likely to otherwise limit or prevent
competition from rivals.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Export Trading Group Australia – pulses and grains business and facility in Mount Tyson, QLD
deals_cleared.merger_description: The acquirer is ETG Queensland Processing Pty Ltd. Export Trading Group Australia Pty Ltd (**ETG Australia**) is the sole shareholder of ETG Queensland Processing Pty Ltd. ETG Australia is a wholesale supplier (including exporter) of certain pulses and grains in Australia.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
ETG Queensland Processing Pty Ltd, a subsidiary of Export Trading
Group Australia Pty Ltd (ETG Australia), proposes to acquire certain
business assets (including land) relating to the operation of a facility
located in Mount Tyson QLD 4356 (Mount Tyson Facility) from
Queensland Cotton Corporation Pty Ltd (Queensland Cotton), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition). ETG Queensland Processing Pty Ltd
applied for a notification waiver in respect of the Acquisition.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
28 April 2026

ETG Australia is the sole shareholder of ETG Queensland Processing
Pty Ltd and is a wholesale supplier (including exporter) of certain
pulses and grains in Australia.
Queensland Cotton operates a business of trading, conditioning,
storing and grading certain pulses and grains and is a wholesale
supplier (including exporter) of certain pulses and grains in Australia.
Queensland Cotton owns and operates the Mount Tyson Facility, a
receival, grading and container packing facility. The Mount Tyson
Facility processes, grades, stores and packs pulses and grains
owned by Queensland Cotton, which Queensland Cotton then
supplies domestically and overseas.
ETG Australia and Queensland Cotton are both wholesale suppliers
of pulses and grains, focussing on chickpeas, mung beans and
sorghum. Queensland Cotton will continue supply of trading,
conditioning, storing and grading of sorghum, as well as other grains
such as barley and wheat.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between ETG Australia
and the pulses and grains business and facility operated by
Queensland Cotton at the Mount Tyson Facility in the supply
of pulses and grains, including chickpeas, mung beans and
sorghum in Australia
b. there are alternative wholesale suppliers of pulses and
grains in Australia.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
Publicis Media Australia Pty Ltd, an indirect wholly owned subsidiary

Australia (advertising, creative, public relations and production
services), Publicis Media Australia (media planning, strategy and
buying services), and Publicis Sapient Australia (digital business
transformation), among others.
160over90 is a marketing agency which forms part of the WME
Group. 160over90 is a global marketing and branding agency
specialising in cultural, sports, and entertainment marketing, with
over 670 employees across the US, UK, EMEA and APAC.
In Australia, 160over90 has a small presence, providing marketing
services, including digital and social media management, event
activation, partnership procurement and activation, public relations
and creative services.
Publicis and 160over90 are both active in the supply of marketing
and communication services. 160over90 does not supply any media
buying services.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver and had regard
to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between Publicis and
160over90 in the supply of marketing and communication
services in Australia and the increase in share of supply
arising from the Acquisition would be small
b. there are alternative providers of marketing and
communication services, including for the cultural, sports,
and entertainment sectors in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

targets.name: China National Aviation Fuel Group Limited

deals_cleared.merger_description: The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (**ACCC**) is currently assessing China Petrochemical Corporation (**Sinopec**)’s proposed acquisition of China National Aviation Fuel Group Limited (**CNAF**), through China’s State-owned Assets Supervision and Administration Commission of the State Council’s transfer of 100% of the equity of CNAF into Sinopec (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Sinopec - CNAF - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
China Petrochemical Corporation (Sinopec) is proposing to acquire
100% of China National Aviation Fuel Group Limited (CNAF) (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Sinopec, is a Chinese stated-owned energy company
that conducts energy investment, natural gas production, petroleum
product trading, lubricant and chemical product sales, and retail fuel
expansion. Sinopec’s activities include jet fuel trading and
marketing.
The target, CNAF, is a Chinese state-owned aviation fuel provider,
which provides purchase, transport, storage, inspection, sales and
into-plane service of aviation fuel. CNAF’s activities are primarily
international. In Australia, CNAF does limited jet fuel trading.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The relevant market to the transaction is jet fuel production and
supply. The parties overlap in the production and supply of jet fuel in
Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The market share aggregation arising from the acquisition is low
and is estimated at less than 10 per cent.
• The merged firm would likely continue to be constrained by
alternative suppliers of jet fuel in Australia (e.g. Ampol, Viva
Energy, Caltex, BP).

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Gainsdale Pty Ltd, Hootie Blowfish Pty Ltd, and Counting Crows Pty Ltd, which are entities associated with Mr Matthew Turner and his family (together, the **Turner Family Entities**), propose to increase their existing shareholdings in Pedal Group Pty Ltd (**Pedal Group**) by collectively acquiring from Flight Centre Travel Group Limited (**Flight Centre**) shares representing (in aggregate) 20.32% of the shares in Pedal Group. In connection with this acquisition, Pedal Group will buy back the remaining shares held by Flight Centre in Pedal Group, representing (in aggregate) 26.68% of the shares in Pedal Group. The share buy-back and proposed acquisition of shares in Pedal Group by the Turner Family Entities (together, the **Acquisition**) are interrelated and conditional upon each other.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1)

determination_table_content.details:
Gainsdale Pty Ltd applied for a notification waiver in respect of:
• the proposed acquisition by Gainsdale Pty Ltd, Hootie
Blowfish Pty Ltd, and Counting Crows Pty Ltd (together, the
Turner Family Entities) of an additional 20.32% of the
shares (in aggregate) in Pedal Group Pty Ltd (Pedal Group)
from Flight Centre Travel Group Limited (Flight Centre)
• Pedal Group’s buy-back of Flight Centre’s remaining shares
in Pedal Group, representing (in aggregate) 26.68% of the
shares in Pedal Group,
as described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).
Upon completion of the Acquisition, the Turner Family Entities’
shareholding in Pedal Group will increase from 36.30% to 77.23%.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
30 April 2026

The acquirers, the Turner Family Entities, are entities associated with
Mr Matthew Turner and his family.
The target, Pedal Group, is a retailer and wholesaler of bicycles, e-
bikes, scooters, associated accessories and a provider of bicycle / e-
bike maintenance services. In Australia, Pedal Group operates 71 ‘99
Bikes’ branded retail stores and the ‘Advance Traders’ wholesale
business. Pedal Group also supplies bicycle rental services in
Queensland.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. aside from their existing interests in Pedal Group, there are
no horizontal overlaps or vertical relationships between the
Turner Family Entities (including their connected entities)
and Pedal Group (including its connected entities) in the
retail and wholesale supply of bicycles, e-bikes, scooters,
associated accessories in Australia or in the supply of bike
maintenance and rental services
b. there are alternative retail and wholesale suppliers of
bicycles, e-bikes, scooters and associated accessories in
Australia
c. there are alternative suppliers of maintenance services and
rental services in areas where Pedal Group supplies such
services.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
MAHF Custodian Pty Ltd, as custodian for RGM TT Pty Ltd (as
trustee for The Entrance Hotel Sub Trust) and Redcape Hotel Group
Pty Ltd, proposes to acquire 100% of the freehold land interest,
buildings, licences and business assets of The Entrance Social Club
located in The Entrance, NSW, as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).
MA Financial Group Limited (MA Financial) applied for a notification
waiver in respect of the Acquisition.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
30 April 2026

The acquirer, MAHF Custodian Pty Ltd, is the custodian for RGM TT
Pty Ltd (as trustee for The Entrance Hotel Sub Trust) and Redcape
Hotel Goup Pty Ltd. The acquirers are part of MA Financial’s
Redcape Fund, which owns and operates community pubs and
hotels. The Redcape Fund portfolio currently has 34 pubs and hotels
in NSW, Queensland and Victoria.
The target, The Entrance Social Club, is a community pub and hotel
offering food, beverage, accommodation, gambling and
entertainment, which is located in The Entrance, NSW. It also
operates a bottle shop, being ‘Bottlemart’.
The parties both supply on-premises and take-away packaged liquor,
dining, gambling, and accommodation services.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is no local horizontal overlap between the Redcape
Fund and The Entrance Social Club in the supply of on-
premises or takeaway packaged liquor, dining, gaming or
accommodation services in The Entrance area or the
surrounding area
b. there are alternative suppliers of on-premises and takeaway
packaged liquor, dining, gaming and accommodation
services at the local and regional level.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.

effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
East Gate Games Investment Company (East Gate), a special
purpose vehicle wholly owned by Saudi Electronic Games Holding
Company (doing business as Savvy Games Group (Savvy)), applied
for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of
100% of the shares of Moonton Holdings Limited (Moonton), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
29 April 2026

The acquirer, East Gate, is a special purpose vehicle wholly owned by
Savvy. Savvy principally invests in and manages companies that
develop, market, and publish games for mobile devices.
Savvy is wholly owned by the Public Investment Fund (PIF), the
sovereign wealth fund of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.
Moonton (a wholly owned subsidiary of Bytedance Ltd. (Bytedance))
is active in mobile game development, publishing and related
business. Bytedance is a holding company registered in the Cayman
Islands.
Savvy and Moonton are involved in the development and publishing
of games for mobile devices, and Savvy is also active in organising
esports events.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. the parties’ combined share in the supply of mobile video
games in Australia is low and the increase in share of supply
arising from the Acquisition would be small
b. there are alternative developers and publishers of mobile
video games in Australia
c. while there is a potential vertical relationship between the
parties in relation to esports, there appears to be a low risk
of foreclosure or other concerning vertical effects resulting
from exclusionary conduct post-acquisition.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: On 5 March 2026, Advanced Navigation Pty. Ltd (**AdNav**), several existing shareholders of AdNav, and several new investors, entered into a sale and subscription deed (**SSD**). Under the SSD, Airtree Ventures Opportunity Fund V Partnership and its affiliates, National Reconstruction Fund Corporation, Australian Retirement Trust, Franklin Park Venture Capital Opportunity Fund II, L.P., LGPE SEC S.C.Sp, Crown Growth Opportunities IV SCSp-RAIF, Crown Asia-Pacific Private Equity V Master SCSp, Crown Asia-Pacific Private Equity VI Alt S.C.Sp., and Harvard Private Capital Realty Inc (collectively, the **New Investors**) along with a variety of other investors will directly or indirectly (through a separate nominee vehicle or trust) acquire shares of AdNav and enter into a shareholders agreement (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Advanced Navigation Pty. Ltd (AdNav), certain shareholders of
AdNav, and certain new investors, entered into a sale and
subscription deed (SSD). Under the SSD, Airtree Ventures
Opportunity Fund V Partnership and its affiliates (Airtree), National
Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRF), a number of co-investors,
along with a variety of other investors propose to directly or
indirectly (through a separate nominee vehicle or trust) acquire
shares of AdNav and enter into a shareholders agreement (the
Acquisition).
Airtree applied for a notification waiver on behalf of the co-investors
and in respect of the co-investors’ acquisition of shares of AdNav.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Airtree is a limited partnership that is managed by Airtree Ventures
Fund V Manager Pty Ltd, an entity which forms part of Airtree
Ventures, a venture capital firm based in Sydney, Australia. Airtree
Ventures primarily focuses on early-stage investments in technology
startups across Australia and New Zealand.
The NRF is an Australian Government specialist investment vehicle,
established in 2023 under the National Reconstruction Fund
Corporation Act 2023. The NRF’s investment function is to provide
finance in the form of debt, equity and guarantees for projects in 7
priority areas of the Australian economy.
The following parties are acquiring shares in AdNav as part of the
Acquisition alongside Airtree and NRF (referred to as co-investors).
• Australian Retirement Trust is one of Australia’s largest
superannuation funds, managing in the order of $350 billion
of retirement savings for more than 2.4 million members and
has a diverse global portfolio.
• Franklin Park Venture Capital Opportunity Fund II, L.P is a
venture capital fund managed by Franklin Park, which is a US
based investment manager and adviser that specialises in
private equity, private debt and venture capital, primarily
through fund‑of‑funds, co‑investment vehicles and bespoke
advisory mandates.
• LGPE SEC S.C.Sp, Crown Growth Opportunities IV SCSp-
RAIF, Crown Asia-Pacific Private Equity V Master SCSp and
Crown Asia-Pacific Private Equity VI Alt S.C.Sp are global
investment firms and are part of the LGT Capital Partners
Group. LGT Capital Partners are involved in managing capital
on behalf of institutional investors, sovereign entities,
insurers, endowments and foundations.
• Harvard Private Capital Realty, Inc is an investment entity
associated with Harvard Management Company, a nonprofit,
wholly owned subsidiary of Harvard University. Its activities
are limited to managing and investing endowment assets
across a diversified portfolio of asset classes.
The target, AdNav, develops, manufacturers and offers AI powered
navigation solutions, primarily for defence primes.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. Airtree, NRF and the co-investors will have a relatively small
interest in AdNav post-Acquisition
b. there is no horizontal overlap, and limited potential vertical
relationships, between the co-investors and their connected
entities and AdNav in the supply of AI powered navigation
software in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Coles Group Limited (**Coles**) is an Australian public company (ASX: COL) that primarily operates:

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Coles Supermarkets Australia Pty Ltd (CSA), a subsidiary of Coles
Group Limited (Coles), applied for a notification waiver in respect of
CSA’s proposed acquisition of a leasehold interest at Lot 3 & 4,
Capricorn Highway, Emerald QLD 4720 from Boulder Capital
Investments Pty Ltd, as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Coles is an Australian public company (ASX: COL) that primarily
operates:
• supermarkets nationwide, through CSA, and
• retail liquor stores nationwide, trading under the Liquorland,
Liquorland Warehouse and Liquorland Cellars brands.
The lessor, Boulder Capital Investments Pty Ltd, is an Australian
private investment company focused on providing debt and equity
into property developments and real estate assets.
The land subject to the proposed lease is located in Emerald, QLD. It
is the proposed site of a new shopping centre comprising of a Coles
supermarket of 2,808 sqm SFA, a 6-bay Click & Collect drive through,
home delivery facilities, 1,200 m2 speciality retail and car parking.
The leasehold interest would be an input to Coles’ supermarket
retailing operations.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, including Coles’
confirmation to the ACCC that it will not operate both supermarkets
on an ongoing basis and the existing store will close as part of the
transition to the replacement store, the ACCC considers that the
Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. the new Coles supermarket will replace Coles’ existing
supermarket, and it will not result in an additional Coles
supermarket, in Emerald, QLD
b. the target land is not currently used for grocery retailing and
Coles’ proposed new supermarket in Emerald will be used to
improve the quality of Coles’ offering in Emerald
c. there are alternative grocery retailers, including Woolworths
and IGA, in the local area.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds determined under section 51ABP(1) being met. The ACCC
does however observe that the targeted notification requirements for
supermarkets determined under section 51ABQ(1) are likely to apply.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

ongoing_phase_1.merger_name: Eli Lilly – acquisition of licence from CSL Behring
ongoing_phase_1.merger_description: Eli Lilly and Company (**Eli Lilly**) and CSL Behring LLC (**CSL Behring**) propose to enter into a Licence Agreement under which Eli Lilly would be granted certain exclusive rights to develop and commercialise Clazakizumab, an anti-interleukin-6 (**IL-6**) monoclonal antibody, for certain new indications including atrial fibrillation, atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and other cardiovascular-related indications (the **Acquisition**). CSL Behring would retain exclusive rights to continue the development and commercialisation of Clazakizumab for the prevention of cardiovascular events in patients with end-stage kidney disease.
